---
title: "Left Behind: The Global Crisis of Out-of-School Adolescents"
author: "Shivani"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    number-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-align: center
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: true
---

# Introduction

Every child deserves an education. Yet across the world, millions of adolescents of
lower secondary school age — children between roughly 12 and 15 years old — are
**not in school**. This report analyses UNICEF data to uncover *where* this crisis
is most acute, *who* it affects most, and *whether* the world is making progress.

The data reveal a stark picture: in **Niger**, nearly **7 in 10 adolescents** are
out of school. Across Sub-Saharan Africa, out-of-school rates routinely exceed
**40–60%**. Meanwhile, in Europe and Central Asia, rates are below **1%**. This is
not simply an education story — it is a story about poverty, geography, and gender.

> *"The right to education is not a privilege for the few — it is a fundamental
> human right. The data show we are still far from fulfilling that promise."*

Key findings at a glance:

-  **115 countries** surveyed across 2010–2020
-  Global average out-of-school rate: **14.2%**
-  Girls face higher exclusion than boys in **every single year** of the dataset
- ️ All top-10 worst countries are in **Sub-Saharan Africa**
-  GDP per capita is the strongest single predictor of school access

---

# Data & Methodology

## Data Sources

This report draws on three UNICEF datasets:

- **Indicator 1** — Out-of-school rate for adolescents of lower secondary school age (%)
- **Indicator 2** — Adjusted net attendance rate for adolescents of lower secondary school age (%)
- **Metadata** — Country-level economic and demographic indicators (GDP per capita,
  life expectancy, population)

## Setup and Libraries

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: true

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import importlib.util
import os
import warnings
import numpy as np
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_map, geom_col, geom_point, geom_smooth,
    geom_line, geom_text, geom_hline, annotate,
    scale_fill_gradient, scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_log10, scale_y_continuous, scale_x_continuous,
    coord_flip, labs,
    theme, theme_minimal, theme_void, theme_classic,
    element_text, element_blank, element_line,
    guides
)

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
```

## Loading Data with Error Handling

```{python}
#| label: load-data
#| echo: true

def load_unicef_csv(path: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """
    Load a UNICEF CSV file into a Polars DataFrame.
    Raises FileNotFoundError with a clear message if the file is missing.
    Prints row/column counts for quick validation on load.
    """
    if not os.path.exists(path):
        raise FileNotFoundError(
            f"Dataset not found at '{path}'. "
            "Ensure all three CSV files are in the same folder as this report."
        )
    df = pl.read_csv(path, infer_schema_length=10000, null_values=["", "NA", "N/A"])
    print(f"  Loaded '{os.path.basename(path)}': "
          f"{df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns")
    return df


print("Loading UNICEF datasets...\n")
indicator1 = load_unicef_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
indicator2 = load_unicef_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
metadata   = load_unicef_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")
print("\nAll datasets loaded successfully.")
```

---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Data Quality Checks

```{python}
#| label: eda-quality
#| echo: true

def summarise_dataset(df: pl.DataFrame, name: str) -> None:
    """
    Print EDA summary for a Polars DataFrame including:
    shape, columns with non-zero null counts, and
    descriptive statistics for all numeric columns.
    """
    print(f"\n{'=' * 58}")
    print(f"  DATASET : {name}")
    print(f"  Shape   : {df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns")
    print(f"\n  Null counts (non-zero only):")
    null_counts = df.null_count()
    any_nulls = False
    for col in null_counts.columns:
        n = null_counts[col][0]
        if n > 0:
            pct = round(100 * n / df.shape[0], 1)
            print(f"    {col:<48}  {n:>5} nulls ({pct}%)")
            any_nulls = True
    if not any_nulls:
        print("    (no nulls in any column)")
    print(f"\n  Numeric summary:")
    print(df.select(pl.col(pl.Float64, pl.Int64)).describe())


summarise_dataset(indicator1, "Indicator 1 - Out-of-School Rate")
summarise_dataset(indicator2, "Indicator 2 - Net Attendance Rate")
summarise_dataset(metadata,   "Metadata - Country Economic Indicators")
```

## Key EDA Findings

The EDA reveals several important facts about the data:

- **Indicator 1** covers **115 countries** from 2010–2020, with observations split
  by sex (Female, Male, Total). The `obs_value` column ranges from near **0%** to
  **72.6%** — capturing the full spectrum of educational access globally.
- **Indicator 2** (net attendance rate) covers 112 countries over the same period.
  Since out-of-school rate and attendance rate are complementary metrics, they
  provide a more complete picture of adolescent education when used together.
- **Metadata** is the most comprehensive file: 211 countries from 1960–2024.
  However, economic indicators such as GDP per capita have significant null counts
  in earlier decades. For the scatter analysis, only rows with non-null
  `gdp_per_capita` are retained, to avoid introducing bias.

---

# Data Transformations

## Reshaping and Joining with Polars

```{python}
#| label: data-transform
#| echo: true

# ── 1. Indicator 1: filter to "Total" sex, cast obs_value, rename year column ─
ind1_total = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(pl.col("obs_value").cast(pl.Float64))
    .rename({"time_period": "year"})
)

# ── 2. Country average OOS rate using group_by ────────────────────────────────
#    group_by + mean ensures each country contributes equally regardless of how
#    many survey years it has, avoiding over-representation of frequently
#    surveyed countries.
country_avg = (
    ind1_total
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(
        pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("obs_value").count().alias("n_obs"),
        pl.col("obs_value").min().alias("min_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("obs_value").max().alias("max_oos_rate")
    )
    .sort("avg_oos_rate", descending=True)
)

# ── 3. Top 10 worst-performing countries for bar chart ────────────────────────
top10 = country_avg.head(10).to_pandas()
top10["label"] = top10["avg_oos_rate"].round(1).astype(str) + "%"
top10["country_display"] = top10["country"].replace(
    {"Tanzania, United Republic of": "Tanzania"}
)

# ── 4. Gender trend by year (group_by on two columns) ────────────────────────
#    Comparing Female vs Male average rates over time reveals whether the
#    gender gap is narrowing, stable, or widening — a key policy metric.
gender_trend = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Female", "Male"]))
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(pl.col("obs_value").cast(pl.Float64))
    .rename({"time_period": "year"})
    .group_by("year", "sex")
    .agg(
        pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("country").n_unique().alias("n_countries")
    )
    .sort("year")
    .to_pandas()
)

# ── 5. Clean metadata: rename long column headers for usability ───────────────
meta_clean = (
    metadata
    .rename({
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":       "gdp_per_capita",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)":  "life_expectancy",
        "Population, total":                        "population"
    })
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "year",
             "gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy", "population"])
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
)

# ── 6. LEFT JOIN: most-recent Indicator 1 row per country <- metadata row ─────
#    Rationale: a LEFT JOIN retains every country that has indicator data, even
#    if the metadata for that exact year is absent. This preserves the widest
#    geographic coverage for the scatterplot rather than silently dropping
#    countries with incomplete economic records.
ind1_latest = (
    ind1_total
    .sort("year", descending=True)
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(
        pl.col("obs_value").first().alias("oos_rate"),
        pl.col("year").first().alias("year")
    )
)

scatter_data = (
    ind1_latest
    .join(meta_clean, on=["country", "alpha_3_code", "year"], how="left")
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col("oos_rate").is_not_null())
    .to_pandas()
)

print(f"Country averages computed for {len(country_avg)} countries.")
print(f"Scatter dataset: {len(scatter_data)} countries with matching GDP data.")
print(f"Gender trend: {len(gender_trend)} year x sex combinations.")
print("\nTop 5 worst-performing countries:")
print(country_avg.head(5).select(["country", "avg_oos_rate", "n_obs"]))
```

---

# Visualisations

## World Map: Geographic Distribution of Out-of-School Rates

*Darker shades of red indicate higher average out-of-school rates.
Grey countries have no data in the UNICEF dataset.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Average out-of-school rate (%) for lower-secondary adolescents by country. Sub-Saharan Africa bears the greatest burden: Niger (69.7%), South Sudan (63.0%), Mali (59.0%), Tanzania (57.0%), and Burkina Faso (57.0%) are the worst affected. Europe, Central Asia, and Latin America show near-zero rates."
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: true

def get_world_shapefile() -> gpd.GeoDataFrame:
    """
    Load the Natural Earth low-resolution world shapefile.
    Tries three sources in priority order:
      1. geopandas bundled dataset (geopandas < 1.0)
      2. pyogrio test fixtures (modern geopandas / pyogrio)
      3. GitHub download fallback (requires internet access)
    """
    # Method 1: geopandas < 1.0 bundled dataset
    try:
        import geopandas.datasets
        return gpd.read_file(gpd.datasets.get_path("naturalearth_lowres"))
    except Exception:
        pass

    # Method 2: pyogrio test fixtures (shipped with modern geopandas)
    spec = importlib.util.find_spec("pyogrio")
    if spec:
        pyogrio_dir = os.path.dirname(spec.origin)
        shp_path = os.path.join(
            pyogrio_dir, "tests", "fixtures",
            "naturalearth_lowres", "naturalearth_lowres.shp"
        )
        if os.path.exists(shp_path):
            return gpd.read_file(shp_path)

    # Method 3: download from GitHub (requires internet)
    import urllib.request, io
    url = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/geopandas/geopandas/main/"
           "geopandas/datasets/naturalearth_lowres/naturalearth_lowres.shp")
    data = urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=15).read()
    return gpd.read_file(io.BytesIO(data))


world = get_world_shapefile()

# Merge using ISO alpha-3 codes (more reliable than country name text matching)
world_merged = world.merge(
    country_avg.to_pandas()[["alpha_3_code", "avg_oos_rate"]],
    left_on="iso_a3",
    right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

(
    ggplot(world_merged)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="avg_oos_rate"), color="white", size=0.15)
    + scale_fill_gradient(
        low="#ffe5c0", high="#b30000",
        na_value="#d0d0d0",
        name="OOS Rate (%)",
        limits=[0, 75]
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "The Geography of Educational Exclusion",
        subtitle = ("Average out-of-school rate for lower-secondary adolescents"
                    " | 2010-2020 | UNICEF"),
        caption  = "Source: UNICEF Indicator 1  |  Grey = no data available"
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title    = element_text(size=14, weight="bold",
                                     color="#1a1a2e", ha="center"),
        plot_subtitle = element_text(size=10, color="#555555", ha="center"),
        plot_caption  = element_text(size=8,  color="#888888"),
        legend_title  = element_text(size=9,  weight="bold"),
        legend_text   = element_text(size=8),
        figure_size   = (11, 6)
    )
)
```

## Bar Chart: The Ten Countries Failing Their Adolescents Most

*Each bar shows the **country average** out-of-school rate across all available
survey years. The dashed blue line marks the global average of 14.2%.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-chart
#| fig-cap: "Top 10 countries by average out-of-school rate for lower-secondary adolescents. Niger's 69.7% is nearly five times the global average of 14.2%. Every single country in this list is in Sub-Saharan Africa — a clear signal that the crisis is geographically concentrated and requires targeted, region-specific policy responses."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: true

(
    ggplot(top10,
           aes(x="reorder(country_display, avg_oos_rate)",
               y="avg_oos_rate",
               fill="avg_oos_rate"))
    + geom_col(width=0.7)
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="label"),
        ha="left", size=9, nudge_y=0.8, color="#333333"
    )
    + geom_hline(yintercept=14.2, linetype="dashed",
                 color="#1a6baf", size=0.8)
    + annotate("text", x=0.8, y=16.5,
               label="Global avg: 14.2%",
               color="#1a6baf", size=8, ha="left")
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#f4a460", high="#8b0000")
    + guides(fill=False)
    + scale_y_continuous(
        limits=[0, 80],
        breaks=range(0, 81, 10),
        labels=lambda l: [f"{x}%" for x in l]
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Top 10 Countries with the Highest Out-of-School Rates",
        subtitle = ("Average across all available survey years"
                    " | Lower secondary adolescents"),
        x        = "",
        y        = "Average Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption  = "Source: UNICEF Indicator 1"
    )
    + theme_classic()
    + theme(
        plot_title        = element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a1a2e"),
        plot_subtitle     = element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        plot_caption      = element_text(size=8,  color="#888888"),
        axis_title_x      = element_text(size=10),
        axis_title_y      = element_blank(),
        axis_text         = element_text(size=10),
        panel_grid_major_x = element_line(color="#eeeeee"),
        panel_grid_minor_x = element_blank(),
        figure_size       = (9, 6)
    )
)
```

## Scatterplot: Wealth Predicts School Access

*Each point = one country's most recent out-of-school rate vs GDP per capita
(log scale). Point size reflects OOS severity. The red line is a **linear
regression** fit with 95% confidence band.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Countries with higher GDP per capita consistently show lower out-of-school rates. The relationship is non-linear (log-scale x-axis): gains are steepest among the poorest nations. Countries below $2,000 GDP per capita frequently show OOS rates above 20-40%, while those above $10,000 cluster near zero. The regression line confirms a strong negative relationship."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: true

(
    ggplot(scatter_data,
           aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="oos_rate"))
    + geom_point(
        aes(size="oos_rate"),
        alpha=0.6, color="#e07b39", show_legend=False
    )
    + geom_smooth(
        method="lm",
        color="#8b0000", fill="#f4a46050", size=1.2
    )
    + geom_hline(
        yintercept=14.2,
        linetype="dotted", color="#1a6baf", size=0.7
    )
    + annotate("text", x=60000, y=17,
               label="Global avg 14.2%",
               color="#1a6baf", size=7)
    + scale_x_log10(
        breaks=[500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000, 30000, 80000],
        labels=["$500", "$1K", "$2K", "$5K", "$10K", "$30K", "$80K"]
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        limits=[0, 75],
        breaks=range(0, 76, 15),
        labels=lambda l: [f"{x}%" for x in l]
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Wealthier Countries Keep More Adolescents in School",
        subtitle = ("GDP per capita (log scale) vs out-of-school rate"
                    " | Most recent survey year per country"),
        x        = "GDP per Capita (constant 2015 USD, log scale)",
        y        = "Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption  = ("Source: UNICEF Indicator 1"
                    " | World Bank via UNICEF Metadata")
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title       = element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a1a2e"),
        plot_subtitle    = element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        plot_caption     = element_text(size=8,  color="#888888"),
        axis_title       = element_text(size=10),
        axis_text        = element_text(size=9),
        panel_grid_minor = element_blank(),
        figure_size      = (9, 6)
    )
)
```

## Time-Series: The Gender Gap Over Time (2010–2020)

*Tracking the **female vs. male** global average out-of-school rate year by year.
Girls are above boys in **every single year** of the dataset.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "Girls are consistently more likely to be out of school than boys across 2010-2020. The female average (14.7%) exceeds the male average (13.6%) in every year. While annual fluctuations partly reflect changing country coverage in the dataset, the female line never falls below the male line — evidence of a persistent, structural gender gap in education access."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| echo: true

(
    ggplot(gender_trend,
           aes(x="year", y="avg_oos_rate",
               color="sex", group="sex"))
    + geom_line(size=1.3)
    + geom_point(size=3)
    + geom_hline(
        yintercept=14.2,
        linetype="dotted", color="#555555", size=0.6
    )
    + annotate(
        "text", x=2019.5, y=15.8,
        label="Overall avg 14.2%",
        color="#555555", size=7
    )
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={"Female": "#c0392b", "Male": "#2980b9"},
        name="Sex"
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2010, 2021)))
    + scale_y_continuous(
        limits=[0, 55],
        breaks=range(0, 56, 10),
        labels=lambda l: [f"{x}%" for x in l]
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Girls Are Consistently More Excluded from School",
        subtitle = ("Global average out-of-school rate by sex"
                    " | Lower secondary adolescents | 2010-2020"),
        x        = "Year",
        y        = "Average Out-of-School Rate (%)",
        caption  = ("Source: UNICEF Indicator 1\n"
                    "Note: Year-to-year variation partly reflects "
                    "changing country coverage in the dataset.")
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title       = element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1a1a2e"),
        plot_subtitle    = element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
        plot_caption     = element_text(size=8,  color="#888888"),
        axis_title       = element_text(size=10),
        axis_text        = element_text(size=9),
        panel_grid_minor = element_blank(),
        legend_title     = element_text(size=9, weight="bold"),
        legend_text      = element_text(size=9),
        legend_position  = "top",
        figure_size      = (9, 5.5)
    )
)
```

---

# The Story: Education Denied

## A Crisis Concentrated in the Poorest Places

The world map makes the regional concentration unmistakable. **Sub-Saharan Africa**
accounts for all ten of the worst-performing countries in the dataset:

| Country | Avg OOS Rate |
|---|---|
| **Niger** | 69.7% |
| **South Sudan** | 63.0% |
| **Mali** | 59.0% |
| **Tanzania** | 57.0% |
| **Burkina Faso** | 57.0% |

In these countries, being out of school is not the exception — it is the *majority
experience* for adolescents. By contrast, countries in Eastern Europe and Central
Asia — Belarus (**0.1%**), Serbia (**0.2%**), Kazakhstan (**0.2%**) — record rates
that are effectively zero. The same human right exists on paper for children in
both regions. The outcomes could not be more different.

## The Economic Driver

The scatterplot reveals what drives so much of this disparity: **poverty**. The
negative relationship between GDP per capita and out-of-school rate is striking:

- Countries below **$2,000 GDP per capita** typically show OOS rates of **20–50%**
- Countries above **$10,000 GDP per capita** cluster near **zero**
- The regression line confirms a strong, statistically robust relationship

Low income creates a cascade of barriers:

- Families cannot afford school fees, uniforms, or materials
- Child labour becomes an economic necessity rather than a choice
- Schools may not exist within a reachable distance
- Teachers are underpaid, undertrained, and in short supply

## The Girl Who Never Got to Go

The time-series chart documents a quieter injustice: **girls are
disproportionately excluded**. Across every year from 2010 to 2020, the female
average out-of-school rate (**14.7%**) exceeded the male rate (**13.6%**). In
the countries where exclusion is highest, this gap is far more severe. Cultural
norms around gender roles, the expectation that girls manage household labour,
and the risk of violence on the journey to school all conspire to push girls out
of the classroom first.

## Attendance Is Not the Same as Learning

Indicator 2 — the **adjusted net attendance rate** — tells a complementary story.
Its global average of **63.3%** means that even among *enrolled* students, a
significant proportion are not regularly attending. The out-of-school crisis is
not only about whether a child can reach a school — it is about whether, once
there, they *remain*.

## Conclusion

The data are clear. Across **115 countries** and a decade of observations, the
same forces — **poverty, geography, and gender discrimination** — determine
whether an adolescent gets an education. The gains made in school enrollment
globally have not reached the most marginalised.

For the world to fulfil its commitment to **SDG 4** (Quality Education for All),
targeted investment in the countries and populations highlighted in this report
is not optional. The children of Niger, South Sudan, and Mali deserve the same
future as the children of Belarus and Kazakhstan. The data show exactly where
to start.

---

# Appendix: Summary Statistics

```{python}
#| label: appendix-summary
#| echo: true

print("=" * 58)
print("  INDICATOR 1: Out-of-School Rate - Numeric Summary")
print("=" * 58)
print(ind1_total.select(["obs_value"]).describe())

print("\n" + "=" * 58)
print("  TOP 10 WORST-PERFORMING COUNTRIES")
print("=" * 58)
print(
    country_avg.head(10)
    .select(["country", "avg_oos_rate", "n_obs",
             "min_oos_rate", "max_oos_rate"])
)

print("\n" + "=" * 58)
print("  GENDER AVERAGES ACROSS ALL YEARS")
print("=" * 58)
gender_summary = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Female", "Male"]))
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .group_by("sex")
    .agg(
        pl.col("obs_value").mean().round(2).alias("mean_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("obs_value").std().round(2).alias("std_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("obs_value").min().round(2).alias("min_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("obs_value").max().round(2).alias("max_oos_rate"),
        pl.col("country").n_unique().alias("n_countries")
    )
    .sort("sex")
)
print(gender_summary)

print("\n" + "=" * 58)
print("  SCATTER DATASET - GDP vs OOS RATE SUMMARY")
print("=" * 58)
print(
    scatter_data[["oos_rate", "gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy"]]
    .describe()
    .round(2)
)
```
